concepts.title: Self Inductance
concepts.conceptBody:
Self Inductance 

When the current in a loop varies with time, the magnetic flux through the conducting loop change with time and an emf is induced in the loop. This is called self induction.

 

$\phi \,\,\,\alpha \,\,B$

$and\quad \,\,B\,\,\alpha \,\,i$

$\therefore \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,$

$\Rightarrow \quad \,\,\,$ $\begin{align} & \text{where L is called the coefficient} \\ & \text{of self inductance.} \\ \end{align}$

Hence emf induced across the inducting loop

\(\mathrm{e}=-\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=-\mathrm{L}\left(\frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)\)

Inductance of a coil depends on:
(a) Geometrical shape and size of the loop

(b) The medium in which the loop is placed.

concepts.title: Self Inductance Due to Circular Coil and Solenoid
concepts.conceptBody:
Self Inductance Due to Circular Coil and Solenoid

Circular Coil

We will find the self inductance for very small circular coil for which the $\vec{B}$ field can be almost assumed to be constant inside the ring.

$\text{B}=\left( \frac{{{\mu }_{0}} i N\text{ }}{2R} \right)$

$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\quad \phi =\left( \frac{{{\mu }_{0}}iN}{2\text{R}} \right)\left( \pi {{\text{R}}^{2}} \right)\text{N}$

$\phi =\left( \frac{{{\mu }_{0}}~{{\text{N}}^{2}}\pi \text{R}}{2} \right)\text{i}$

$\therefore \quad \,\,\,\,\text{L}=\left( \frac{{{\mu }_{0}}\pi }{2}R{{N}^{2}} \right)$

Solenoid 

$B\,\,\,=\left( {{\mu }_{0}}ni \right)$

 

$\begin{align} & \phi \,\,=\left( {{\mu }_{0}}ni \right)\left( \pi {{R}^{2}} \right)\,\,N \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,=\left( {{\mu }_{0}}nN\pi {{R}^{2}} \right)\,\,i \\ \end{align}$

$\phi \,\,=\left( \frac{{{\mu }_{0}}~{{N}^{2}}\pi {{R}^{2}}}{1} \right)i\quad here\,\,n\,\,is\,\,the\,\,number\,of\,turns\,per\,unit\,length\,\,n=\frac{N}{l}$

concepts.title: Mutual Inductance
concepts.conceptBody:
Mutual Inductance

When the current in the primary loop varies with time the magnetic flux through the secondary loop changes with time hence an emf is induced across the secondary coil. This is called mutual induction.

$\begin{align} & {{\phi }_{\text{s}}}~\alpha~ {{\text{i}}_{\text{p}}} \\ & \therefore   M= \text{ coefficient of mutual inductance } \\ & \text{ Emf induced in secondary coil, } \\ \end{align}$ 
$\left\{ \begin{matrix} \text{ where }\text{ }{{\phi }_{\text{s}}}\text{ is the flux linked with secondary } \\ \text{ coil and } {{i}_{\text{p}}}\text{ is current in primary coil } \\ \end{matrix} \right\}$

$\in =-\frac{d{{\phi }_{\text{s}}}}{dt}=-M\left( \frac{d{{i}_{\text{p}}}}{dt} \right)$

Mutual inductance of two coils depends on the:
(a) Geometrical size and shape of the loops 
(b) Relative orientations of the loop 
(c) The medium in which they are placed.

concepts.title: Mutual Inductance of Two Coils
concepts.conceptBody:
Mutual inductance of two coils 

It is given by $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

where ${{L}_{1}},~{{L}_{2}}$ are the inductances of two coils

In series combination, when mutual inductance of the coil is taken in consideration

${{L}_{\text{eq}}}\,\,=\,\,{{L}_{1}}+{{L}_{2}}\,\,\pm \,\,\,2M$

${{\text{L}}_{\text{eq}}}={{\text{L}}_{1}}+{{\text{L}}_{2}}+\,\,2\text{M}$ 

 

${{\text{L}}_{\text{eq}}}=\,\,{{\text{L}}_{1}}+{{\text{L}}_{2}} \,-\,\,2\text{M}$ 

Note : When the current is increasing in inductor, the induced emf opposes the flow of current and hence is induced in the opposite direction of current flow.
